What's up everybody? So there are two ways to do this whole longer form style. Now mind you, within fitness it doesn't necessarily mean it's truly long form, but it would just be a little longer than normal and be more of a content or a give. So the two ways to do it are as follows. The first is you can still simply optimize for conversions on content that is still relevant as long as there's a call to action at the end. And there's a lot of times that that still works. It's just changing your mindset around providing value in the advertisement rather than a pure direct response ad. So that's option one and that's obviously the simplest way. The second way to do it is to do it as follows, which is you have your initial value ad, which has a soft pitch. And then what you do is when you're actually making the advertisement, you're going to be trying to increase reach. And so the campaign objective is going to be impressions or video views. And so the point is that we're trying to get as many eyeballs to watch x percent of the video, in this case 75% or more of the video. Now, after that, you're going to retarget the person with a direct response ad. So that's where you have your, you have your content piece first, and then if someone sees over 75% of it, then you would retarget them with a direct response ad. I use both of these strategies. And so I actually do conversion ads on the front end for our content pieces. And then I retarget only with a more direct advertisement to people who watched over x percent. So that's how I do it. You can do whichever way you want. Ultimately, it's going to be a testing thing. So just be aware of anyone who's like, it's got to be this way. It's like, you just have to look at the numbers. Sometimes it works really well. And a lot of it, as always, is going to be dependent on the creative and the copy in the audience. And so some ads do really well, some ads don't, some ads that I run for content just start crushing it on the just from straight from the soft pitch component. Some of them that are longer, I get, I re-cut most of my ads spent on the direct response ad, that's the retargeting ad based on the people who did XYZ. So they both work. And if it's like, which way do I do, you try both. And you turn off the ones that don't work. And that just is welcome to market. You know what I mean? Like that's just how that works. Below this, you'll have kind of the tactics of how to set this up, how to set up the audience. Don't get overly like twisted about it. It's simply defining like you're asking Facebook to make an audience of the people who did XYZ. And then when you're selecting for your direct response ad, you're just only going to select that audience. And so you don't even need to select a radius for that. You can literally just select the audience because that audience will be comprised of people who were already pre selected for the radius. So it's actually really simple to set up the retargeting. The more complex and amusing quotes here, because it's still not that hard. Part is to set up the initial audience. So once you set up the initial audience, all you do then is you make your next ad. That's the direct response ad. And the audience that it is targeting is the one that you just created. The beauty of that is that it's dynamic. And what I mean by that is, it means that that audience continues to get updated. So I think I'm not mistaken, it's 180 days. So people will stay in an audience for 180 days on Facebook for a given action. So it's not like you can retarget them for the rest of their lives. But for 180 days, after they watch the 75%, they will be in that audience and you can continue to hit them with a little bit more right hook style ads. One last piece is you see the last line here says run new ads at $5 per day to the audience is that when you're running to a remarketed audience, the audience size will be significantly smaller, like way smaller. So, you know, let's see, figure you spend $200 and you get a reach of 10,000, 10,000 people saw this, 10,000 impressions. And then let's just say 3% of those people actually, if it was 75%, probably you heard that's probably be like, let's say 10% of the people hit 75% of the video. So that means that you've got 100 people who are going to enter this new audience. Now remember your CPM is your cost per impression. That means we have 50 people who are sorry, 100 people who are in this audience, right? So you've 100 people in this tiny new audience, a CPM cost for 1000 is usually 20 bucks. And so if you want to reach those 100 people, you only need to spend like four bucks a day, you know, I mean, it's not a lot. And so that's why we're targeting can be very profitable because anything you're milking from that group costs you so little to reach them because they're highly targeted. The downsides is it's not, you're not going to get tons and tons and tons and tons, but the quality of those applications will be higher. And so that's the flip side. The reason that it's even a good strategy to begin with is just that it helps you soak up more from your initial people who are seeing the ad and clicking over. The advantage that we have in a local marketplace is that you can still probably just run the straight content as a conversion ad, and you'll still probably be okay because you have trust. I don't have trust in my marketplace. And so I have to do a little bit more beforehand to get more, if that makes sense. So that's the overarching strategy from a targeting standpoint. There's two ways to run it. One is straight conversion from a content piece. The other is running the content piece for video views and then retargeting only the people who watch 75% with a true direct response ad. That's the game and that's how it works. And the video blog will show you how to actually do it.
